author: @shawnbishop9199
text: I like the concept but your resistor the way you present it shows some safety concerns. It's essentially an electrolysis tank. You say your a chemist so I'm sure your aware of electrolysis and the byproducts of it. Using table salt for your electrolytic solution with steel electrodes produces chlorine gas. Also wouldstrongly recommend against using stainless steel for your electrodes as it produces a very toxic gas. also whatever electrolytic solution or electrodes you use it should always be done outdoors in a ventilated area. Just wondering how well it would do with just wondering how well it would do with sodium carbonate and steel electrodes so then the only byproduct is hydrogen and oxygen. I'm sure proximity and surface area would both play a role in the effectiveness as does I'm sure in your concept.

author: @CXensation
text:
A good old wellknown trick is to use a length of galvanised water piping approx 1m.
The galvanised steel pipe acts as a variable power resistor by length.
Adjsuting the welding current is then very simple by connecting one clamp at end of pipe and the other clamp somewhere between the pipe ends to suit your needed welding current.
NOTE !
The pipe gets HOT after some time of usage, because the principle here is to regulate the current by wasting power.

author: @michaelszczys8316
text:
He says “ normally you put the negative on the workpiece”  normally is negative electrode where I’ve been from for the last 40 years. He has it hooked up the “ normal “ way.
I’ve tried doing this with batteries a few times over the years. Batteries have lots of amperage but batteries ( bat-trees ) are flat DC with no arcing power. 30 years ago I hooked up two batteries in series ( 24 volt ) and tried welding with small 7014 rod but it wouldn’t arc at all. Then I hooked up a battery charger to one of the batteries which then gave it a little pulse and helped it to arc enough to weld two pieces of angle iron together in a nice weld. One of my batteries was old and after an inch it ran out of steam.
About 25 years ago I tried it again this time I had two good batteries but only a 4 amp charger so the best I could get was some ‘ scrubbing ‘ welds with almost no arc. Enough to tack weld a motorcycle frame together which I then took to my job and MiG welded.
If the rod stuck it would light up like a toaster in 3 seconds.
Does his ‘ water rheostat ‘ help it to arc somehow?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: positive is on the workpiece mate - but I am told you can do either way - it seems everyone has his or her advice and they also seem to conflict - so my guess is it really doesn't matter that much

replies.author: @michaelszczys8316
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith  The way the electrons are flowing indeed make tremendous difference in welding. The heat being concentrated on the work or on the electrode.
Whenever I see someone hooking a battery ( or even a few ) to a welding rod and striking a nice continuous arc I am indeed very skeptical as I have tried that numerous times and it just doesn’t work that way. If I see someone do it I want to know how. Seriously.
I thought up a form of rapid switching to possibly create some kind of inductance and help it to arc but I haven’t tried it yet.

author: @snoddyification
text:
Thanks. 
Depending on the gauge of the work, decides polarity. 
Thin=<5mm =electrode neg.
Thick=>5mm =electrode pos.
Like you , not sure why.
Rod guage selection also adds to the variable.
Awesome, Thanks again.

replies.author: @hanelyp1
replies.text: The polarity influences how much heat is put in the work vs. the electrode.  The positive pole gets more heat.  I has to do with electrons in the arc moving much faster than ions, carrying heat with them to the positive end of the arc.

author: @andyweb7779
text:
Now... if that salt water were to tip over would that be seriously dangerous? 
And also because that's 12-48V would it kill you or just give you a serious dig?

replies.author: @gregraynard781
replies.text: Water and electricity are a hazard. But so it electricity. If yu manage to put yourself BETWEEN any two potentials, high voltage OR high amperage can kill you. Between is the key operative, as standing in a pool of salt water with a live cable will not harm you until your body gives the potential somewhere to go. Lips on a microphone can kill if the mic has a ground potential thats different than any other point (like the guitar players strings, the rainwater on the stage. etc. The current moves thru one limb to another (yes your head is on a limb) by way of the artieries, being they are the best conductors in your body. When this happens, its called "electrocution", your heart is the connecting tissue and death is possible. Sticking your tongue on a battery is unpleasant but not going to kill you unless the battery somehow has made contact somewhere other than your tongue, this is called "shock". Electrocution can kill and your heart is in the middle, never, never hold electrodes one in each hand. Use a ground clip for safety, this allows you to stay out of the circuit. Be respectful of electricity, be aware that it can kill but fear it no more than a circular saw. It is good practice to work with one hand behind your back whenever you are  in a potentally (pun intended) deadly situation. High frequency voltage travels along the surface more and more and the frequency goes up. With HF a small amount of current can kill. With low frequency like AC mains 50 or 60 Hz, a small voltage will penetrate tissues easily, so in that case a small AC Voltage can be lethal. Just dont be a jackass. If you climb on 30,000 Volt high tension lines you are in the most danger when you are near another object. Touching 30,000 Volts or 30,000 Amps will not kill you unless you give the potential a place to go (see lightening show, Faraday cage). Do not test what i have shared. Use it for safety, and if you understand it well, share it with others. Also, if you ever find yourself "frozen" like they always show in the movies, unlock your knees and let gravity pull you free. If you see someone getting electrocuted, do not grab them, as you may find yourself part of the same circuit, instead shut the power off. If you have to physicly intervene then use a insulating material like wood, a dry blanket, cardboard, and knock the person loose. Only tackle someone if there are no other options.

author: @FullSpeedAhead19
text: The misconception that DC is safer than AC don't understand how it works.  Because it is a wave you have the ability to let go during a shock unlike DC that is constant would not allow you to let go in case of a shock... Also AC has very quick  short circuit controls on the breaker to keep you safe.  This is a great experiment but no substitute for a true AC welding machine.

author: @bridgendesar
text: Can it be done without a welding rod, or is that outer sheath critical?

replies.author: @snoddyification
replies.text:
In short YES.
As a weld is formed, atmospheric gasses infiltrate the weld zone.
These gasses cause weld perosity if not mitigated. Weld failure ensues.
The role of the powder is to create it's own protective atmosphere in the zone, displacing atmospheric gasses.
A mig welder uses bottled gas to do same.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @aion2177
text:
very good info - you saved me :)) 
In a remote location with no electricity, I have a 1kw generator which can't power the 6kW welder, but maybe it can slowly charge a battery which then can let me weld in return. So i don't need a welder, and i don't need a big generator - both of which are expensive, i can just use a battery and this cheap generator i have, which is relatively cheap overall and accomplish almost the same thing. 
I can accomplish the exact same thing if i hookup an Amp-meter which will indicate the amps exactly like on the welding device :D
I did not actually test this but i think will work.
Very cool :) Thanks :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i'm sure it will work mate - if one battery isn't enough try two - but it will definitely work - cheers for the post

replies.author: @manaquriazertyuio4555
replies.text:
you could add a 555 oscillator and a bunch of transistors in an H bridge to get a high frequency ac. (or you could use an arduino instead of the 555 with a high current shunt to also set the amperage)
though I'm not sure this is a good idea long term since lead acid batteries used at high current will degrade fairly quickly.
using supercapacitors might be better.
another option is to use a couple of truck alternators in parallel, and run those off of an engine. (2 24V100A alternators in parallel, or 2 12V200A in series.) you should be able to find a pair from a junkyard for less than 200 bucks. and a 6-10 hp engine for less than 100. you should also use a resistor (or the arduino I talked about previously) to limit the current to under 200A.

replies.author: @davemwangi05
replies.text: @manaquriazertyuio4555 Can't the 6KW generator work using the salt shunt? I can't see why it wont.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: For those interested, there is a difference in welds when using ac vs dc.   Current in the weld stick and work piece can interact with current through the puddle, pushing metal to a side.  Just something to look into to better understand and better control ones welds.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: 5 years ago i hooked up 3 car batteries in series and welded my hub on my landcruiser in the middle of outback nsw on the side of the road. I rolled into town half hour later to the cheers of local farmers at the pub . They said they had heard of it but never seen it done ! So they all gathered around my truck umming and arring and asking me all sorts of question . A great day to share a beer🍺  i explaind it was better to have 4 batteries as it will have greater arc and draw far lower amps. I think some went home early that day to go try it forthemselves 👍🏻

I love that truck and travelled so many places in it !

Is there some sort of chem reaction going on?  Because i really didn't scrub it like so many YouTube vids show and doubt it works by scrubbing away the stickiness.
